food_subgroup: Long-grain fragrant white rice, raw uncooked

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice grain, milled white, long-grain, fragrant (jasmine)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw jasmine rice as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for specific raw grain varieties is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dry serving of jasmine rice is approximately half a cup (90g raw), which yields roughly 200–210g of cooked rice after absorption cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Jasmine Rice (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05090015; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Rice, white, long-grain, raw, unenriched, FDC ID 169756)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F05090015 is an exact match for raw jasmine rice as consumed in Singapore. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white long-grain raw rice (FDC ID 169756). All core macros and micronutrients are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Jasmine rice (steamed, cooked); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 73 is reported for cooked (steamed) jasmine rice per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Raw rice does not have a directly measurable GI as it is not consumed raw; the GI value is attributed to the cooked form and is the standard reference used for nutritional labelling and dietary guidance for raw jasmine rice. Glycemic load of 52.6 is calculated as (73 × 72g available carbohydrate per 90g serving) / 100 = 52.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5mg/100g raw); no direct contribution to elevated blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Jasmine rice is essentially sodium-free at 5mg per 100g raw and is safe for individuals with hypertension when prepared without added salt; portion control remains advisable to manage overall caloric and carbohydrate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~73) due to high amylopectin starch content; rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit jasmine rice to 100–150g cooked per meal, substitute at least one daily serving with lower-GI alternatives such as basmati rice (GI ~58), parboiled rice (GI ~47), or brown rice (GI ~55), and consider cooling and reheating cooked rice to increase resistant starch content and reduce effective GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High glycemic load with frequent large-portion consumption linked to insulin resistance and dyslipidaemia; very low saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While jasmine rice is very low in saturated fat (0.1g/100g) and sodium, its high glycemic index and glycemic load with habitual large-portion consumption (2–3 servings/day) is associated with elevated triglycerides and insulin resistance; cardiovascular patients should moderate portion sizes and diversify grain intake with whole grains.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis at standard serving levels; not a significant source of pro- or anti-inflammatory compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Refined white jasmine rice is nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis at standard serving sizes; high glycemic load diets are weakly associated with systemic inflammation, so patients with active disease may benefit from substituting some servings with whole grain alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jasmine rice (raw, uncooked) is the long-grain fragrant white rice that serves as the primary dietary staple of Singapore, Thailand, and most of Southeast Asia. The raw grain is hard, dry, and semi-translucent with a characteristic natural floral aroma derived from 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline. Milling removes the bran and germ layers, leaving a grain composed predominantly of starch (approximately 80g carbohydrate per 100g) with modest protein (7.1g) and negligible fat (0.5g). When cooked by the absorption method, jasmine rice becomes soft, moist, and slightly sticky due to its high amylopectin content. The high amylopectin-to-amylose ratio is also responsible for its elevated glycemic index of approximately 73 (high GI), making it one of the highest-GI staple grains. Raw jasmine rice provides 362 kcal per 100g and is very low in sodium (5mg), dietary fibre (0.4g), and micronutrients compared to whole grain alternatives. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. A standard dry serving is approximately 90g (½ cup dry), yielding roughly 200–210g cooked. For individuals with type 2 diabetes, portion control and substitution with lower-GI grains is strongly recommended. Jasmine rice is used across all Singaporean cuisines and is the base grain for dishes such as Hainanese chicken rice, nasi lemak, and everyday hawker centre meals.
common_user_queries: how many calories in raw white rice
is jasmine rice good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Exact food record found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under Food Code F05090015, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for white long-grain raw rice. All core macros and key micronutrients are consistent between both approved primary sources. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for raw jasmine rice is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo Database F05090015 entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reports combined fat of 0.58g with monounsaturated 0.18g and polyunsaturated 0.16g for generic white long-grain raw rice, but these values are not confirmed in the HPB source and are therefore conservatively set to -1 pending source reconciliation. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for raw jasmine rice is not reported in the HPB Foodo F05090015 entry; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 11.6g water per 100g for raw white long-grain rice, but this is not confirmed in the primary HPB source. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F05090015 and USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — jasmine rice is a plant-based grain and contains no cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F05090015 — raw milled white rice contains negligible amounts of these nutrients. GI value of 73 applies to cooked (steamed) jasmine rice; raw rice GI is not directly measurable as it is not consumed in raw form. Glycemic load of 52.6 calculated as (73 × 72g available carbohydrate per 90g serving) / 100.
